skill.name: matplotlib
skill.description: A low-level plotting library for comprehensive customization. Use when fine-grained control over every plot element is needed, creating new types of charts, or integrating into specific scientific workflows. Can export to PNG/PDF/SVG for publication. For quick statistical charts, use seaborn; for interactive charts, use plotly; for journal-style, publication-ready multi-panel charts, use scientific-visualization.
skill.summary: Guided access to Matplotlib expertise with ready-to-run scripts, comprehensive reference documentation, and best practices for publication-quality visualizations.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 566 static analysis findings are false positives. The 511 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' findings are markdown code block fences in documentation files, not actual shell commands. The 'weak cryptographic algorithm' and 'certificate/key files' findings are triggered by hex color codes and matplotlib keyboard event references. The 'system reconnaissance' findings are matplotlib introspection examples and interactive CLI input() calls. The 'network' findings are legitimate documentation URLs. The heuristic combination findings aggregate these false positives. This is a legitimate matplotlib skill with plotting scripts and reference documentation.

content.value_statement: Creating polished, publication-ready visualizations requires precise control over every plot element. This skill provides guided access to Matplotlib expertise with ready-to-run scripts and comprehensive reference documentation.
actual_capabilities: Create line, scatter, bar, histogram, heatmap, contour, box, violin, and 3D plots
Customize plot appearance with style sheets, colormaps, and rcParams
Generate multi-panel layouts using subplots, GridSpec, and mosaic layouts
Export plots to PNG, PDF, and SVG formats with DPI control
Configure and save reusable matplotlib style configurations
Troubleshoot common display, layout, and formatting issues
limitations: Does not handle large-scale data preprocessing or statistical modeling
Interactive plotting features require a display server and are not headless
Does not create animated or real-time streaming visualizations
Not optimized for web-based interactive charts

use_cases.title: Research Publication Figures
use_cases.description: Generate publication-ready multi-panel figures with precise styling, annotations, and high-resolution export.

use_cases.title: Data Analysis Reporting
use_cases.description: Create standardized charts for data analysis reports with consistent styling and formatting.
use_cases.target_user: Data analysts and business intelligence professionals

use_cases.title: Presentation Visuals
use_cases.description: Produce presentation-ready charts with large fonts, accessible colormaps, and clean layouts.

prompt_templates.title: Create a basic line plot
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a line plot showing [dataset description]. Use matplotlib with the pyplot interface. Add axis labels, a title, and a legend. Save the plot as a PNG file.

prompt_templates.title: Design a multi-panel figure
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a multi-panel figure with [N] subplots using matplotlib. Each subplot should show a different chart type. Use the object-oriented interface with constrained_layout. Apply consistent styling across all subplots. Export at 300 DPI.
prompt_templates.scenario: Publication-ready figure with multiple views of the data

prompt_templates.title: Apply custom color scheme and style
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a matplotlib figure with custom style configuration. Use a [viridis/plasma/coolwarm] colormap. Customize font sizes, spine visibility, grid style, and legend appearance through rcParams. Add annotations to highlight key features. Export as PDF with tight bounding box.

prompt_templates.title: Build a reusable plotting workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a reusable matplotlib plotting function that accepts data, style parameters, and output format as arguments. The function should support multiple plot types, apply consistent styling, handle error bars, and support faceted layouts. Include docstrings and type hints.
prompt_templates.scenario: Standardized plotting for repeated analysis workflows

output_examples.input: Create a line plot of monthly sales data showing trend and seasonal patterns for the last two years.
output_examples.output: A line plot with two overlaid yearly trend lines, labeled axes, a legend distinguishing the years, grid lines for readability, and annotations highlighting peak sales months. The chart uses a clean publication style with a title and footnote showing data source.

output_examples.input: Generate a multi-panel figure comparing four distributions using box plots and histograms.
output_examples.output: A 2x2 grid of plots with box plots showing distribution quartiles and outliers in the top row, and histograms with density overlays in the bottom row. Each panel has consistent axis labels, matching color schemes, and shared axis ranges for direct comparison.

output_examples.input: Create a heatmap of the correlation matrix for these 10 financial indicators.
output_examples.output: A heatmap displaying pairwise correlations with a diverging colormap, annotated correlation values in each cell, and a colorbar legend. The diagonal shows perfect self-correlation. Cell colors transition from cool (negative) through neutral (zero) to warm (positive).

best_practices: Use the object-oriented interface (fig, ax) instead of pyplot state machine for production code
Always specify figsize and DPI at figure creation time for consistent output dimensions
Use constrained_layout or tight_layout to prevent axis label and tick overlap
anti_patterns: Using pyplot global state in functions that need to be reusable or testable
Relying on implicit figure creation instead of explicitly creating and managing figure objects
Using non-perceptually-uniform colormaps like jet that can misrepresent data patterns

faq.question: What is the difference between pyplot and object-oriented interfaces?
faq.answer: Pyplot is a state-based interface similar to MATLAB that manages a global figure state. The object-oriented interface gives direct control over Figure and Axes objects for complex multi-panel layouts.

faq.question: How do I export high-resolution figures for publication?
faq.answer: Use plt.savefig with dpi=300 and bbox_inches='tight'. For vector output use PDF or SVG format. Set facecolor='white' for a white background.

faq.question: Which colormap should I use for my data?
faq.answer: Use sequential colormaps (viridis, plasma) for ordered data, diverging (coolwarm, RdBu) for data with a center point, and qualitative (tab10, Set3) for categorical data.

faq.question: How do I prevent text overlap in my figure?
faq.answer: Enable constrained_layout=True when creating the figure, or call fig.tight_layout() before saving. These adjust spacing automatically.

faq.question: Can I use this skill without a display server?
faq.answer: Yes, matplotlib can save figures to files without a display. The scripts use savefig() which works in headless environments. Interactive display requires a display server.

faq.question: How do I customize the style of all plots consistently?
faq.answer: Use matplotlib rcParams to set global defaults, or create a custom .mplstyle file using the style_configurator.py script included in this skill.
